coredata.dc:title: Yellow-emission and pH-responsive carbon dots employed for “turn-off” and “turn-off-on” assaying adenosine triphosphate and kanamycin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Lu
Liu, Jie
Bai, Yunfeng
Feng, Feng
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a simple and moderate strategy for the determination of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and kanamycin (Kana) has been established, which was achieved by employing the yellow-fluorescence carbon dots derived from cholesterol and o-phenylenediamine (CDs@Chol) through one-pot hydrothermal treatment. Meanwhile, CDs@Chol were equipped with the functional groups of -CC-, -OH, -NH2 and -CO, providing the opportunity of interacting with the potential targets. Significantly, the fluorescence of CDs@Chol was quenched by introducing ATP and further recovered by Kana, and the “turn-off” and “turn-off-on” sensing platform were accordingly built for detecting ATP and Kana with a generally acceptable reliability. To be specific, the “turn-off” mode was applied to detect ATP with a detection limit of 93 nM based on photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and the assay of “turn-off-on” was raised to determine Kana (LOD of 7.2 μM) by virtue of the hydrogen bonds forming, which may broaden the avenues of sensing both ATP and Kana.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Yellow-fluorescence
Carbon Dots
Determination
Adenosine triphosphate
Kanamycin

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ATP and Kana by CDs@Chol
Fluorescent response of CDs@Chol to ATP and Kana
Optimizing the conditions of CDs@Chol detecting ATP and Kana
Establishing the strategy of detecting ATP and Kana
Mechanism of CDs@Chol assaying ATP and Kana

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of synthesizing CDs with cholesterol and o-diaminobenzene for assaying ATP and kanamycin.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs@Chol, Inset: images of CDs@Chol with daylight and UV; (B) and (C) Emission spectra of CDs@Chol with different excitations; (D) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs@Chol, (E) HR-TEM image and (F) FTIR spectrum of CDs@Chol.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@Chol with various pH (2.0–7.0); (B) Images of CDs@Chol in acidic conditions with daylight and UV light; (C) Plot of fluorescence intensity of CDs@Chol versus pH.

Fluorescence spectra of ATP or CDs@Chol without and with ATP, Inset: images of CDs@Chol, ATP and CDs@CHol with ATP under UV light (A); Fluorescence intensity of CDs@Chol with ATP for different incubation time (B) or temperature (C); Fluorescence spectra of CDs@Chol with various concentrations of ATP, Inset: F/F0-1 versus varied concentrations of ATP (D); Fluorescence variation of CDs@Chol in presence of ATP or other compounds (E), CDs@Chol with ATP in presence of ions or other compounds (F).

Fluorescence spectra of CDs@Chol, CDs@Chol with ATP, CDs@Chol with both ATP and Kana, Inset: images of CDs@Chol, CDs@Chol with ATP, CDs@Chol with both ATP and Kana with UV light (A); Fluorescence intensity of CDs@Chol with ATP and Kana for different incubation time (B) or temperature (C); Fluorescence spectra of CDs@Chol and ATP with various concentrations of Kana, Inset: F/F0-1 versus varied concentrations of Kana (D); Fluorescence variation of CDs@Chol in presence of ATP and Kana or other compounds (E), CDs@Chol with ATP and Kana in presence of ions or other compounds (F).

(A) Absorption of ATP, fluorescence excitation and emission of CDs@Chol; (B) Fluorescent lifetime of CDs@Chol, CDs@Chol with ATP; (C) LUMO and HOMO of CDs@Chol and ATP; (D) FTIR of CDs@Chol, CDs@Chol with ATP; (E) FTIR of CDs@Chol with ATP, CDs@Chol with both ATP and Kana; (F) Size distributions of CDs@Chol, CDs@Chol with ATP, CDs@Chol with both ATP and Kana.

head.ce:title: Yellow-emission and pH-responsive carbon dots employed for “turn-off” and “turn-off-on” assaying adenosine triphosphate and kanamycin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a simple and moderate strategy for the determination of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and kanamycin (Kana) has been established, which was achieved by employing the yellow-fluorescence carbon dots derived from cholesterol and o-phenylenediamine (CDs@Chol) through one-pot hydrothermal treatment. Meanwhile, CDs@Chol were equipped with the functional groups of -CC-, -OH, -NH2 and -CO, providing the opportunity of interacting with the potential targets. Significantly, the fluorescence of CDs@Chol was quenched by introducing ATP and further recovered by Kana, and the “turn-off” and “turn-off-on” sensing platform were accordingly built for detecting ATP and Kana with a generally acceptable reliability. To be specific, the “turn-off” mode was applied to detect ATP with a detection limit of 93 nM based on photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and the assay of “turn-off-on” was raised to determine Kana (LOD of 7.2 μM) by virtue of the hydrogen bonds forming, which may broaden the avenues of sensing both ATP and Kana.

Herein, a simple and moderate strategy for the determination of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and kanamycin (Kana) has been established, which was achieved by employing the yellow-fluorescence carbon dots derived from cholesterol and o-phenylenediamine (CDs@Chol) through one-pot hydrothermal treatment. Significantly, the fluorescence of CDs@Chol was quenched by introducing ATP and further recovered by Kana, and the “turn-off” and “turn-off-on” sensing platform were accordingly built for detecting ATP and Kana with a generally acceptable reliability.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, carbon dots (CDs) with several nanometers in diameter play a critical role in the field of fluorescent nanomaterials and show the promising prospect with their attractive luminescence, favorable photo-stability, acceptable biocompatibility and satisfactory water-solubility [1–4]. Accordingly, these properties potentiate their wide applications of bioimaging [5], sensing [6], photocatalysis [7], anti-bacteria [8], drug delivery [9], light-emitting diodes [10] and more. Thus far, diversified strategies mainly including hydrothermal, ultrasound/microwave, electrochemical, thermal decomposition or plasma treatment have been developed to synthesize CDs [11–15]. Meanwhile, natural materials and chemical substances such as fruits, vegetables, plants, proteins, amino acids, pure compounds could serve as the carbon source of CDs [16–19]. Especially, CDs exhibited the abundant functional groups on their surfaces, resulting in their multiple interactions with the potential targets, and thus showed the obvious superiority towards sensing various ions, antibiotics and beyond. For instance, a strategy based on carbon dots was proposed for the sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ and glutathione. Again, carbon dots were synthesized at room temperature and served as the fluorescence probe for assaying tetracycline hydrochloride [20,21].

Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), considered as one kind of primary cellular energy, plays the crucial role during numerous biological processes mainly including triphosphoric acid cycle, glycolysis and DNA synthesis [22–24]. In the process of metabolism, ATP could be hydrolyzed as adenosine diphosphate (ADP) and orthophosphate (Pi) through ATPase catalysis. Followed by the further catalyzing, ADP could be hydrolyzed as adenosine monophosphate (AMP) and another Pi, which is regarded as the critical energy source of organism [25]. Substantial evidence has been provided that the fluctuating level of ATP for human being is closely associated with the diseases of ischemia, cardiovascular disease or hypoglycemia [26,27]. Even, the varying concentrations of ATP reflect the cell active state [28,29]. Hence, it shows valuable to monitor the amounts of ATP for living organism, and developing the accurate and facile methods is of great importance for detecting ATP.

Kanamycin (Kana) is well known as one routine member of aminoglycoside antibiotics [30]. Owing to its prominent effect of inhibiting bacteria, Kana has been widely utilized to treat the bacterial infections in the fields of agriculture, livestock husbandry and aquaculture [31]. Nevertheless, abusing Kana causes the growth of Kana resistance and the abundant accumulation in the milk and meat products, and the continuous intake of these products may result in the nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity or hepatotoxicity towards human health [32,33]. Thus, developing appropriate methods to monitor the amount of Kana is meaningful.

Up to date, various techniques for the quantitative analyses of ATP and Kana have been reported mainly consisting of colorimetric method [34,35], high performance liquid chromatography [36,37], mass spectrometry [38,39] and electrochemical sensor [33,40]. However, the intrinsic disadvantages of time-consuming, sophisticated equipment and operation indeed restricted their broader applications. Benefited from the simple synthesis procedures and satisfactory optical performance, carbon dots have been emerging as one critical member of sensors towards kinds of detections. For example, a strategy for the detection of ATP was established with ethanolamine acting as the precursor of CDs [41]. Again, carbon dots combined with layered MoS2 were employed to detect Kana by FRET forming [42].

Herein, one kind of yellow-fluorescence CDs was originally synthesized with the precursors of cholesterol and o-diaminobenzene through a hydrothermal way ( Fig. 1A), and their surfaces were equipped with the groups of -CC-, -OH, -NH2 and -CO. Furthermore, the fluorescence of CDs responded to varying pH values, which exhibited their potentiality of sensing pH. Importantly, introducing ATP into CDs promoted their fluorescence quenching owing to the electron transfer from CDs to ATP. Besides, the further addition of Kana induced the formation of hydrogen bonds, recovering their fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 1B). Thus, the proposed CDs here provided a new way of assaying ATP and Kana.

Basically, the metal ions including Ba2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Pb2+, K+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Na+, sodium hydroxide, methyl alcohol, acetone, ethyl alcohol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dichloromethane, acetonitrile, methanal, tetrahydrofuran (THF), glucose, procaine, thiamine, ascorbic acid, metformin, L-Tryptophan as well as warfarin were purchased from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Similarly, quinine sulfate and lovastatin were obtained from Meryer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Again, cholesterol, tobramycin, folic acid, acetaminophen, glutathione (GSH), gentamycin sulfate, adenosine triphosphate (ATP) were acquired from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Then, kanamycin was purchased from Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and o-phenylenediamine (OPD), isoniazide and urea were bought from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Besides, norfloxacin, ibuprofen, acyclovir and indomethacin were obtained from yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. as well as Britton-Robinson buffer solution (BR buffer) from Acmec Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Further, the ultrapure water of 18.25 MΩ·cm produced by an UPT-I ultrapure water system (Chengdu, China) was employed during the whole experimental process.

All the fluorescence intensity was measured with Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), and the size distributions were identified by Zetasizer nano dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, England). Simultaneously, the elementary composition and functional groups analysis were performed by ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (Waltham, America) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. Also, High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were acquired with a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Columbia, America) at 200 kV. Then, the ultraviolet diffuse reflection was accomplished by a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), and the reduction potentials were tested with Chenhua electrochemical workstation of CHI1030A (Shanghai, China). Then, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was captured by a FLS1000 spectrometer (Edinburgh, England), and the UV–vis absorption spectra was obtained by using a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), and a high-speed centrifuge TG16-W (Changsha, China) was introduced for separations. Additionally, the water bath of DF-101 s (Gongyi, China) provided various temperature, and detecting pH value was achieved by Fangzhong pHS-3 C digital pH meter (Chengdu, China). Again, synthesizing CDs@Chol was finished by muffle furnace of ZSX 1200 (Beijing, China), and the powder of CDs was obtained through the lyophilization by PiloFD8–4.3 V (Charlotte, USA).

The yellow-fluorescence of CDs@Chol was originally prepared here. In brief, 3.0 mL of ethyl alcohol and 3.0 mL of ultrapure water were successively added into 10 mL of centrifuge tube, and 0.5060 g of cholesterol and 0.1012 g of o-phenylenediamine were therewith introduced accompanied by the continuously stirring. Subsequently, the previous mixture was transferred into 25 mL of Teflon reaction kettle followed by reacting for 8 h at 180 ◦C. Through cooling, the reaction product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to separate from the precipitation, and the supernatant was collected and filtered by 0.22 µm of filtration membrane for removing the large particles. Furthermore, the obtained CDs@Chol was dialyzed with ultrapure water for 5 h in dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da), and the purified product was subjected to lyophilization and stored at 4 ◦C for the following application.

During the process of exploring the fluorescence stability of CDs@Chol towards pH, 400 μL of ultrapure water and 100 μL of CDs@Chol (2 mg/L) were added into the parallel centrifuge tubes (1.5 mL) along with stirring. Then, 500 μL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer with various pH values (2−7) was separately introduced into the previous mixtures followed by the incubation at 35 ℃ for 20 min, and the corresponding intensity of fluorescence was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ATP and Kana by CDs@Chol
ce:section.ce:para: During the process of detecting ATP by CDs@Chol, 100 μL of CDs@Chol (2 mg/L) and different concentrations of ATP (1 ×10−2-10−7 mol/L, 100 μL for each) were parallelly pipetted into several centrifuge tubes (1.5 mL), and all the mixtures above were diluted with 800 μL of ultrapure water. Next, these mixtures were incubated at 25 ℃ (pH=7) for 20 min followed by measuring their fluorescence intensity at 387 nm. Towards assaying Kana, 100 μL of CDs@Chol (2 mg/L) and 100 μL of ATP (10−2 mol/L) were pipetted into the parallel centrifuge tubes (1.5 mL). Then, 100 μL of Kana with various concentrations ( 1 ×10−2-10−6 mol/L) as well as 700 μL of ultrapure water were introduced, and these solutions were incubated with 10 min at 35 ℃ (pH=7), and their related fluorescence intensity was accordingly determined.

To clarify their property of CDs@Chol, the systematic characterizations were detailedly performed. Particularly, CDs@Chol appeared as faint yellow with daylight ( Fig. 2 A, picture a), and emitted the yellow fluorescence with UV light (Fig. 2A, picture b), and showed the optimal emission of 556 nm excitated by 387 nm. Meanwhile, CDs@Chol exhibited the varied fluorescence intensity by different excitations, indicating their excitation-dependent feature (Fig. 2B and C), and their fluorescence lifetime was calculated to be 1.83 ns (Fig. 2D). To explore the morphology of CDs@Chol, the HR-TEM image and DLS showed their homogeneous dispersion with an average diameter of 3.5 ± 0.8 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm (Fig. 2E and Fig. S1A).

To identify the functional groups of CDs@Chol, their FTIR spectrum was acquired. As exhibited in Fig. 2F, there existed the stretching vibrations of -OH (3312.8 cm−1), -NH2 (3202.85 cm−1), -CO (1635.66 cm−1), -CC (1500.64 cm−1), -C-N (1226.75 cm−1) and -C-O (1132.23 cm−1) as well as the bending vibration of -CH2 (2928.96 cm−1). Again, the peaks of 230 nm and 280 nm of UV–vis spectrum for CDs@Chol revealed the π-π∗ transition of CC and CO (Fig. S1B). Furthermore, the corresponding survey of XPS was obtained, showing three major elements of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s (Fig. S2A). In particular, the spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. S2B) described three forms of C binding for 284.18 eV (C-C), 284.86 eV (CC) and 288.32 eV (CO). Likewise, there emerged two peaks for N 1 s (Fig. S2C), being ascribed to 398.76 eV (C-N) and 400.42 eV (N-H). Also, the deconvolution of O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2D) exhibited three peaks of 531.23 eV (CO), 532.06 eV (C-OH) and 533.05 eV (C-O). Taken together, the results of XPS for CDs@Chol showed an agreement with that of FTIR, demonstrating the existence of -OH, -NH2, -CO and -CC on their surfaces.

Besides, the quantum yield CDs@Chol was calculated on the basis of the following equation, while their absorbance (lower than 0.05 at 387 nm) of CDs@Chol and quinine sulfate were employed [43]:
(1)
ϕ C = ϕ s
A c × P s × ( η C ) 2
A s × P C × ( η S ) 2

For the equation, the subscripted C and S respectively represent CDs@Chol and quinine sulfate, and A as well as P denote for the integrated fluorescence intensity and corresponding absorbance, while η serves as the refractive index. Accordingly, the quantum yield of CDs@Chol was calculated to be 15.28% (Fig. S3).

To achieve their optimal fluorescence of CDs@Chol, the related synthesis conditions were investigated. As exhibited in Fig. S4, their fluorescence intensity of CDs@Chol was more or less influenced by the reaction time, temperature and mass-ratio of OPD to cholesterol, and thus 8 h of reaction, 180 ◦C and the mass-ratio of 1: 5 for OPD to cholesterol were selected as the optimized conditions of synthesizing CDs@Chol.

To explore the fluorescence stability of CDs@Chol upon different conditions, the further experiments were designed and performed. In brief, there scarcely emerged the fluorescence variation of CDs@Chol with diverse temperature, time, concentrations of NaCl and metal ions (Fig. S5A, S5B, S5C, S5E). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@Chol showed the slight change in different organic solvents of methanol, methanal, acetone, dichloromethane, DMSO, acetonitrile and THF (Fig. S5F). In striking contrast, the fluorescence of CDs@Chol displayed the obvious variation towards different pH values (Fig. S5D). Specifically, their fluorescence obviously responded to acidic values (pH=2–7), but barely varied in alkaline conditions (pH=8–12). Generally, the as-prepared CDs@Chol showed the satisfactory fluorescence stability.

During investigating their fluorescence stability for various pH, CDs@Chol exhibited the regular response to acidic pH values. In particular, the fluorescence gradually increased with pH from 2 to 7 ( Fig. 3A), and the corresponding images of CDs@Chol with daylight and UV light were displayed (Fig. 3B), providing the linear range of CDs@Chol sensing acidic pH values with the correlation coefficient of 0.953 (Fig. 3C).

To understand the principle of CDs@Chol sensing acidic pH, the zeta potentials and UV–vis absorption of CDs@Chol with different pH values (2−7) were recorded. As indicated in Fig. S6A, the zeta potentials of CDs@Chol gradually changed from 33.2 to − 19.6 mV. Simultaneously, and their UV–vis absorption showed the red shift at 230 nm and 280 nm along with pH increasing from 2–7 (Fig. S6B). The findings revealed that the protonation and deprotonation of the nitrogenous surface for CDs@Chol lead to their fluorescent variation of towards varying pH [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of CDs@Chol to ATP and Kana
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the advantaged fluorescence of CDs@Chol, we thus addressed that whether CDs@Chol could serve as the fluorescence probe or not. Interestingly, the fluorescence of CDs@Chol reduced around 66% in the presence of ATP (10−3 mol/L), providing their possibility of assaying ATP ( Fig. 4A). Likewise, the fluorescence of CDs@Chol, ATP, and CDs@Chol with ATP were recorded under UV light (Fig. 4A, a, b, c). Additionally, their fluorescence partly recovered after introducing Kana of 10−4 mol/L ( Fig. 5A), and the associated images with UV light were supplied (Fig. 5A, a, b, c), showing the prospect of CDs@Chol assaying Kana.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the conditions of CDs@Chol detecting ATP and Kana
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the accurate determination of ATP, multiple factors that may interfere the detection were explored. Initially, the incubation time of CDs@Chol with ATP (10−3 mol/L) varying from 0 to 70 min was studied, and their fluorescence showed the maximum decrease at 20 min (Fig. 4B). Likewise, the incubated temperature of 20–50 ◦C was tested, while the 25 ◦C was selected as the appropriate temperature (Fig. 4C). Besides, the incubations with varied pH values from 3 to 10 were also studied, and pH= 7 was selected as the optimal detecting condition (Fig. S7A). Thereby, 20 min, 25 ◦C and pH= 7 served as the optimal conditions for the process of CDs@Chol assaying ATP. Analogously, the similar experiments were conducted to explore the optimal conditions of CDs@Chol detecting Kana (10−3 mol/L). As shown in Fig. 5B, C and S7B, 10 min, 35 ◦C and pH= 7 were verified as the optimized measuring conditions for Kana.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishing the strategy of detecting ATP and Kana
ce:section.ce:para: Through the previous explorations, the analytical strategies of ATP and Kana were built up. Basically, the fluorescence of CDs@Chol linearly reduced with the introduction of different concentrations of ATP (from 2 ×10−6 to 8 ×10−3 mol/L). Thus, the linear relationship of F0/F-1 versus varying concentrations of ATP was displayed with a correlation coefficient of 0.995 (Fig. 4D), and the detection limit was calculated as 9.3 × 10−7 mol/L. Morevoer, the fluorescence of CDs@Chol with ATP recovered by further adding Kana with a linear range of 10−5 to 4 × 10−3 mol/L, while the detection limit was 7.2 × 10−6 mol/L (Fig. 5D). Consequently, an innovative and applicable method of determining ATP and Kana by CDs@Chol was successfully raised here.

To identify the selectivity of CDs@Chol sensing ATP, several medicines including glucose, thiamine, ascorbic acid, metformin, L-Tryptophan, urea, glutathione, warfarin, acyclovir, procaine as well as lovastatin were introduced. Specifically, the results illustrated that other medicines rather than ATP barely showed effect on the fluorescence of CDs@Chol (Fig. 4E), indicating the generally satisfactory selectivity towards ATP. Similarly, there appeared scarce fluorescence change of CD@Chol with ATP in presence of different medicines (gentamicin, indomethacin, isoniazid, urea, tobramycin, paracetamol, procaine, acyclovir, ribavirin, warfarin and norfloxacin), and only Kana obviously recovered their fluorescence (Fig. 5E), demonstrating that CDs@Chol with ATP showed the favorable selectivity for Kana. In addition, the anti-interference ability for assaying both ATP and Kana by CDs@Chol was investigated by employing various potential ions and substance (Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, paracetamol, folic acid, glucose, ribavirin, acyclovir, allocaine, warfarin, ibuprofen and isoniazide). Accordingly, the results described the acceptable anti-interference of CDs@Chol assaying ATP and Kana (Figs. 4F and 5F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs@Chol assaying ATP and Kana
ce:section.ce:para:
To elucidate the principle of CDs@Chol detecting ATP, the further experiments were designed and performed. Primarily, the absorption of ATP hardly overlapped with the fluorescent spectra (excitation and emission) of CDs@Chol, thus excluding the occurrence of inner filter effect ( Fig. 6A). Subsequently, dynamic quenching effect (DQE) or static quenching effect (SQE), which may lead to their quenching of CDs@Chol with ATP, was therewith explored by the equation of Stern-Volmer [46]:
(2)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
C
q
= 1 +
K
q
τ
0
C
q

Here, F
0 and F respectively represented the fluorescence of CDs@Chol without or with ATP; and C
q indicated the concentration of ATP as well as K
sv for the dynamic quenching constant. As shown in Fig. 4D, K
sv was calculated to be 2.31 mM−1 according to the slope of the linear equation. Also, K
q as well as τ
0 represented the quenching rate constant and fluorescence lifetime of CDs@Chol (τ0 = 1.83 ns), respectively. Since Kq of DQE is usually less than 1.0 × 1010 M−1 S−1 [47], and the K
q here calculated as 1.26 × 1012 M−1 S−1 through K
sv was higher than that of DQE, indicating that the current quenching was originated from SQE rather than DQE. Besides, the lifetime of CDs@Chol (τ
0 = 1.83 ns) scarcely varied compared with that of CDs@Chol with ATP (τ
0 = 1.93 ns), further confirming the fluorescence quenching by SQE (Fig. 6B) [48].

Furthermore, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of CDs@Chol and ATP were calculated through the following formulas [49,50]:
(3)
E
HOMO
= −
e
E
ox
+
4.4
(4)
E
LUMO
= −
e (
E
red
+ 4.4 )

Particularly, E
ox and E
red separately stood for the oxidation and reduction potentials, and the E
red of CDs@Chol and ATP by cyclic voltammetry (CV) were measured as − 0.75 eV and − 0.65 eV, thus their related E
LUMO were calculated to be − 3.65 eV and − 3.75 eV (Fig. 6C). Because of the nonreversible oxidation behavior, their E
HOMO could not be obtained from formula of (3). Accordingly, the identification of E
HOMO could be acquired through E
red and the optical energy band gap (E
g obtained by the absorption edge of absorption spectra) [49,50]:
(5)
E
HOMO
=
E
LUMO
−
E
g
40

As exhibited in Fig. S8A and S8B, the E
g of CDs@Chol and ATP were obtained as 3.99 eV and 5.31 eV, and thus their E
HOMO were calculated to be − 7.64 eV and − 9.06 eV by the equation of (5), respectively. Taken together, the excited electrons of LUMO for CDs@Chol transferred to the LUMO of ATP, thus resulting in their fluorescence quenching of CDs@Chol. These results demonstrated that the mechanism of CDs@Chol sensing ATP could be clarified by photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [46,51]. Moreover, the FTIR spectrum of CDs@Chol with ATP exhibited a broad peak at 3414.78 cm−1 compared with that of CDs@Chol only (Fig. 6D), describing the formation of hydrogen bonds, which facilitated the occurrence of PET [52].

Besides, the mechanism for the fluorescence recovering of CDs@Chol was also explored. To be specific, the FTIR spectrum of CDs@Chol with both ATP and Kana showed the red shifted of C-NH2 (from 3202.48 cm−1 to 3237.59 cm−1, Δσ=35) and C-OH (from 3336.27 cm−1 to 3359.34 cm−1, Δσ=23) by comparing with that of CDs@Chol with ATP (Fig. 6E), thus leading to the formation of hydrogen bonds between ATP and Kana [47], which indeed affected the PET occurring between CDs@Chol and ATP. Besides, the size distribution of CDs@Chol with ATP showed lager than that of CDs@Chol, and the average diameter of CDs@Chol with both ATP and Kana existed as the largest (Fig. 6F), further confirming the previous findings of the hydrogen bonds forming.

On the whole, we here proposed a simple and convenient approach of synthesizing CDs originated from cholesterol and OPD. Meanwhile, the prepared CDs@Chol showed the yellow-fluorescence with the favorable stability, and were mainly equipped with the groups of amino and hydroxyl on their surfaces. Meaningfully, the fluorescence of CD@Chol linearly responded to pH values (from 2 to7), promoting their potential application of sensing pH. Significantly, introducing ATP into CDs@Chol lead to their fluorescence quenching, and the further addition of Kana dramatically recovered their fluorescence, thus CDs@Chol have been employed for achieving the quantitative detections of both ATP and Kana. In addition, the quenching mechanism of CDs@Chol assaying ATP was clarified as photoinduced electron transfer, and their fluorescence recovery was attributed to the hydrogen bonds between ATP and Kana. Overall, the as-prepared CDs@Chol may provide an innovative strategy for monitoring the amounts of ATP and Kana.

Lu Zhao & Jie Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Yunfeng Bai: Software, Validation. Feng Feng: Review & editing, Resources. Xiaoming Yang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi (201801D121035), Cultivate Scientific Research Excellence Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2020KJ023), Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2020–133).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.127640.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
